"Sapphire Dragon Key Problem v17.19"

  description: "At precisely 20:20, the Sapphire Dragon Key vanished from a locked case in the southern archives. Four suspects (1, 2, 3, 4) had recorded badge data near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements; the other three are fully truthful. Only one suspect is truly guilty. Each suspect provides three statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I left the archives at 20:10, so I was not there at 20:20.'
      (2) 'If 2 is lying, then 3 definitely stole the Key.'
      (3) 'Suspect 4 is not guilty; I saw them in a different hall at 20:15.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) 'Suspect 1 stayed until 20:25, contradicting 1’s claimed 20:10 departure.'
      (2) 'If 3 is truthful, then 4’s statements must be false.'
      (3) 'I was never near the Dragon Key after 20:05, so I cannot be guilty.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I did not approach the locked case after 20:00.'
      (2) 'Either 2 is the culprit, or 1 lied about their departure time—but not both.'
      (3) '4 is absolutely innocent; I glimpsed them leaving early.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) 'Suspect 3 was near the archives at 20:05, contradicting 3’s denial.'
      (2) 'If 2 is lying, then 1 must be telling the truth about 20:10.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty; the real thief is 2 or 3, for sure.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Sapphire Dragon Key? Provide only one digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal Logic Approach:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all statements,' T(x) = 'x tells truth in all statements,' and G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one suspect has L(x)=true, exactly one suspect is G(x)=true, the others T(x).

  Case L(1):
    Then 1’s (1),(2),(3) are false => 1 might not have left 20:10, or might be guilty. (2) false => if 2 is lying => 3 definitely stole Key is untrue => no forced scenario. (3) false => 4 is not definitely innocent => 4 can be guilty. Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => 2(1) '1 stayed until 20:25' is correct => conflict with 4(2) etc. Contradiction arises.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2’s (1),(2),(3) are false => (1) false => 1 indeed left at 20:10. (2) false => if 3 is truthful => 4 must be false does not hold => 4 can be truthful. (3) false => 2 was near the Key after 20:05 => possible guilt. Then 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(3) '4 not guilty' => G(4)=false => 3(2) 'Either 2 is culprit or 1 lied time, not both' => 1 didn't lie => 2 is culprit => no contradiction. So L(2)=>G(2).

  Case L(3):
    Then 3’s (1),(2),(3) are false => (1) false => 3 approached after 20:00 => possible guilt. (2) false => 'Either 2 is culprit or 1 lied time not both' fails => might be both or neither => confusion. (3) false => 4 not absolutely innocent => 4 might be culprit. But 1,2,4 are truthful => times lead to conflict with 2(1). Contradiction.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4’s (1),(2),(3) are false => 1,2,3 truthful => times or blame produce mismatch especially with 2(2) 'If 3 is truthful => 4 statements false'... we get contradiction. Fails.

  Only L(2) scenario is consistent => G(2). The real thief is suspect 2."

  final: "2"
